question_text: A spherical ball of salt is dissolving in water in such a manner that the rate of decrease of the volume at any instant is proportional to the surface. Prove that the radius is decreasing at a constant rate.
question_html: A spherical ball of salt is dissolving in water in such a manner that the rate of decrease of the volume at any instant is proportional to the surface. Prove that the radius is decreasing at a constant rate.
solution_text: We have, rate of decrease of the volume of spherical ball of salt at any instant is proportional to surface area. Let the radius of the spherical ball of the salt be r. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the ball (V) {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}{/tex} and surface area (s) {tex} = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\because \frac{{dV}}{{dT}} \propto s{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right) \propto 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac43\pi 3{r^2}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \propto 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \propto \frac{{4\pi {r^2}}}{{4\pi {r^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k.1{/tex} [where, k is the proportionality constant] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} Hence, the radius of ball is decreasing at a constant rate.
solution_html:
We have, rate of decrease of the volume of spherical ball of salt at any instant is  proportional to surface area. Let the radius of the spherical ball of the salt be r.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the ball (V) {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}{/tex}
and surface area (s) {tex} = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\because \frac{{dV}}{{dT}} \propto s{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right) \propto 4\pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac43\pi 3{r^2}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \propto 4\pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \propto \frac{{4\pi {r^2}}}{{4\pi {r^2}}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k.1{/tex} [where, k is the proportionality constant]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k{/tex}
Hence, the radius of ball is decreasing at a constant rate.

question_text: If the area of a circle increases at a uniform rate, then prove that perimeter varies inversely as the radius.
question_html: If the area of a circle increases at a uniform rate, then prove that perimeter varies inversely as the radius.
solution_text: Let the radius of circle = r and area of the circle, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dt}}(A) = \frac{d}{{dt}}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{d}}{{dt}}(A) = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(i) Since, the area of a circle increases at a uniform rate, then {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}(A) = k{/tex} ...(ii) Where, k is a constant. From Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = \frac{k}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{k}{{2\pi }}.\left( {\frac{1}{r}} \right){/tex} ...(iii) Let the perimeter, {tex}P = 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}.2\pi r \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2\pi r.\frac{k}{{2\pi }}.\frac{1}{r} = \frac{k}{r}{/tex} [Using Eq. (iii)] {tex} = \frac{{dP}}{{dt}}\alpha \frac{1}{r}{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
Let the radius of circle = r and area of the circle, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dt}}(A) = \frac{d}{{dt}}\pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{d}}{{dt}}(A) = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(i)
Since, the area of a circle increases at a uniform rate, then
{tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}(A) = k{/tex} ...(ii) Where, k is a constant.
From Eqs. (i) and (ii),
{tex}2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = k{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = \frac{k}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{k}{{2\pi }}.\left( {\frac{1}{r}} \right){/tex}...(iii)
Let the perimeter, {tex}P = 2\pi r{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}.2\pi r \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2\pi r.\frac{k}{{2\pi }}.\frac{1}{r} = \frac{k}{r}{/tex} [Using Eq. (iii)]
{tex} = \frac{{dP}}{{dt}}\alpha \frac{1}{r}{/tex} 
Hence proved

question_text: A kite is moving horizontally at a height of 151.5 meters. If the speed of kite is 10 m/s, how fast is the string being let out; when the kite is 250 m away from the boy who is flying the kite? The height of boy is 1.5 m.
question_html: A kite is moving horizontally at a height of 151.5 meters. If the speed of kite is 10 m/s, how fast is the string being let out; when the kite is 250 m away from the boy who is flying the kite? The height of boy is 1.5 m.
solution_text: We have, height {tex}\left( h \right) = 151.5m/s,{/tex} speed of kite {tex}\left( v \right) = 10m/s{/tex} Let CD be the height of kite and AB be the height of boy. Let {tex}DB = xm = EA{/tex} and {tex}AC = 250m{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 10m/s{/tex} From the figure, we see that {tex}EC = 151.5 - 1.5 = 150m{/tex} And {tex}AE = x{/tex} Also, {tex}AC = 250m{/tex} In right angles {tex}\triangle CEA{/tex} , {tex}A{E^2} + E{C^2} = A{C^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + {\left( {150} \right)^2} = {y^2}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + {\left( {150} \right)^2} = {\left( {250} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} = {\left( {250} \right)^2} - {\left( {150} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}= \left( {250 + 150} \right)\left( {250 - 150} \right){/tex} {tex} = 400 \times 100{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = 20 \times 10 = 200{/tex} From Eq. (i), on differentiating w.r.t t, we get {tex}2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 0 = 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{200}}{{250}}.10 = 8m/s{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 10m/s} \right]{/tex} So, the required rate at which the string is being let out is 8 m/s.
solution_html:
We have, height {tex}\left( h \right) = 151.5m/s,{/tex} speed of kite {tex}\left( v \right) = 10m/s{/tex} 
Let CD be the height of kite and AB be the height of boy.
Let {tex}DB = xm = EA{/tex} and {tex}AC = 250m{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 10m/s{/tex} 

From the figure, we see that
{tex}EC = 151.5 - 1.5 = 150m{/tex} 
And {tex}AE = x{/tex} 
Also, {tex}AC = 250m{/tex} 
In right angles {tex}\triangle CEA{/tex},
{tex}A{E^2} + E{C^2} = A{C^2}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + {\left( {150} \right)^2} = {y^2}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + {\left( {150} \right)^2} = {\left( {250} \right)^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} = {\left( {250} \right)^2} - {\left( {150} \right)^2}{/tex} 
{tex}= \left( {250 + 150} \right)\left( {250 - 150} \right){/tex} 
{tex} = 400 \times 100{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore x = 20 \times 10 = 200{/tex} 
From Eq. (i), on differentiating w.r.t t, we get
{tex}2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 0 = 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{200}}{{250}}.10 = 8m/s{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 10m/s} \right]{/tex} 
So, the required rate at which the string is being let out is 8 m/s.

question_text: Two men A and B start with velocities v at the same time from the junction of two roads inclined at {tex}{45^o}{/tex} to each other. If they travel by different roads, find the rate at which they are being separated.
question_html: Two men A and B start with velocities v at the same time from the junction of two roads inclined at {tex}{45^o}{/tex} to each other. If they travel by different roads, find the rate at which they are being separated.
solution_text: Let two men start from the point C with velocity v each at the same time. Also, {tex}\angle BCA = {45^o}{/tex} Since, A and B are moving with same velocity v, so they will cover same distance in same time. Therefore, {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} is an isosceles triangle with AC = BC. Now, draw {tex}CD \bot AB{/tex} Let at any instant t, the distance between them is AB Let AC = BC = x and AB = y In {tex}\triangle ACD{/tex} and {tex}\triangle DCB{/tex} , {tex}\angle CAD = \angle CBD{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because AC = BC} \right]{/tex} {tex}\angle CDA = \angle CDB = {90^o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \angle ACD = \angle DCB{/tex} or {tex}\angle ACD = \frac{1}{2} \times \angle ACB{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \angle ACD = \frac{1}{2} \times {45^o}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \angle ACD = \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{AD}}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{y/2}}{x}\,\left[ {\because AD = \frac{y}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{2} = x\sin \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = 2x\sin \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2.\sin \frac{\pi }{8}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 2.\sin \frac{\pi }{8}.v\,\left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= 2v.\frac{{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } }}{2}\,\,\left[ {\because \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } }}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } {/tex} v unit /s which is the rate at which A and B are being separated.
solution_html:
Let two men start from the point C with velocity v each at the same time.
Also, {tex}\angle BCA = {45^o}{/tex} 
Since, A and B are moving with same velocity v, so they will cover same distance in same time.
Therefore, {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} is an isosceles triangle with AC = BC.
Now, draw {tex}CD \bot AB{/tex} 
Let at any instant t, the distance between them is AB

Let AC = BC = x and AB = y
In {tex}\triangle ACD{/tex} and {tex}\triangle DCB{/tex},
{tex}\angle CAD = \angle CBD{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because AC = BC} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\angle CDA = \angle CDB = {90^o}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \angle ACD = \angle DCB{/tex} 
or {tex}\angle ACD = \frac{1}{2} \times \angle ACB{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \angle ACD = \frac{1}{2} \times {45^o}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \angle ACD = \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{AD}}{{AC}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{y/2}}{x}\,\left[ {\because AD = \frac{y}{2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{2} = x\sin \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow y = 2x\sin \frac{\pi }{8}{/tex} 
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2.\sin \frac{\pi }{8}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}= 2.\sin \frac{\pi }{8}.v\,\left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= 2v.\frac{{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } }}{2}\,\,\left[ {\because \sin \frac{\pi }{8} = \frac{{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } }}{2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } {/tex} v unit /s
which is the rate at which A and B are being separated.

question_text: Find an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} Where {tex}0 < \theta < \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} , which increases twice as fast as its sine.
question_html: Find an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} Where {tex}0 < \theta < \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}, which increases twice as fast as its sine.
solution_text: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} increases twice as fast as its sine. {tex}\Rightarrow \theta = 2\sin \theta {/tex} Now, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get {tex}\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = 2.\cos \theta .\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} \Rightarrow 1 = 2\cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} So, the required angle is {tex}\frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} increases twice as fast as its sine.

{tex}\Rightarrow \theta = 2\sin \theta {/tex}

Now, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get

{tex}\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = 2.\cos \theta .\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} \Rightarrow 1 = 2\cos \theta {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \theta = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex}

So, the required angle is {tex}\frac{\pi }{3}{/tex}.

question_text: Find the approximate value of {tex}{\left( {1.999} \right)^5}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the approximate value of {tex}{\left( {1.999} \right)^5}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let x = 2 and {tex}\Delta x = - 0.001\,\left[ {\because 2 - 0.001 = 1.999} \right]{/tex} let {tex}y = {x^5}{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 5{x^4}{/tex} Now, {tex}\Delta y = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\Delta x = 5{x^4} \times \Delta x{/tex} {tex} = 5 \times {2^4} \times \left[ { - 0.001} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 80 \times 0.001 = - 0.080{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left( {1.999} \right)^5} = y + \Delta y{/tex} {tex}= {2^5} + \left( { - 0.080} \right){/tex} {tex}= 32 - 0.080 = 31.920{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x = 2

and {tex}\Delta x = - 0.001\,\left[ {\because 2 - 0.001 = 1.999} \right]{/tex}

let {tex}y = {x^5}{/tex}

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 5{x^4}{/tex}

Now, {tex}\Delta y = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\Delta x = 5{x^4} \times \Delta x{/tex}

{tex} = 5 \times {2^4} \times \left[ { - 0.001} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = - 80 \times 0.001 = - 0.080{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {\left( {1.999} \right)^5} = y + \Delta y{/tex}

{tex}= {2^5} + \left( { - 0.080} \right){/tex}

{tex}= 32 - 0.080 = 31.920{/tex}

question_text: Find the approximate volume of metal in a hollow spherical shell whose internal and external radii are 3 cm and 3.0005 cm, respectively.
question_html: Find the approximate volume of metal in a hollow spherical shell whose internal and external radii are 3 cm and 3.0005 cm, respectively.
solution_text: Let internal radius = r and external radius = R {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of hollow spherical shell, {tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {{{\left( {3.0005} \right)}^3} - {{\left( 3 \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) Now, we shell use differentiation to get approximate value of (3.0005) 3 . Let {tex}{\left( {3.0005} \right)^3} = y + \Delta y{/tex} and {tex}x = 3,\Delta x = 0.0005{/tex} Also, Let {tex}y = {x^3}{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 3{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \Delta y = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \times \Delta x = 3{x^2} \times 0.0005{/tex} {tex}= 3 \times {3^2} \times 0.0005{/tex} {tex} = 27 \times 0.0005 = 0.0135{/tex} Also, {tex}{\left( {3.0005} \right)^3} = y + \Delta y{/tex} {tex}= {3^3} + 0.0135 = 27.0135{/tex} {tex}\therefore V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {27.0135 - 27.000} \right]{/tex} [using Eq. (i)] {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {0.0135} \right] = 4\pi \times \left( {0.0045} \right){/tex} {tex} = 0.0180\pi c{m^3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let internal radius = r and external radius = R

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of hollow spherical shell, {tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {{{\left( {3.0005} \right)}^3} - {{\left( 3 \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)

Now, we shell use differentiation to get approximate value of (3.0005)3.

Let {tex}{\left( {3.0005} \right)^3} = y + \Delta y{/tex}

and {tex}x = 3,\Delta x = 0.0005{/tex}

Also, Let {tex}y = {x^3}{/tex}

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 3{x^2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \Delta y = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \times \Delta x = 3{x^2} \times 0.0005{/tex}

{tex}= 3 \times {3^2} \times 0.0005{/tex}

{tex} = 27 \times 0.0005 = 0.0135{/tex}

Also, {tex}{\left( {3.0005} \right)^3} = y + \Delta y{/tex}

{tex}= {3^3} + 0.0135 = 27.0135{/tex}

{tex}\therefore V = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {27.0135 - 27.000} \right]{/tex} [using Eq. (i)]

{tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi \left[ {0.0135} \right] = 4\pi \times \left( {0.0045} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 0.0180\pi c{m^3}{/tex}

question_text: A man, 2m tall, walks at the rate of {tex}1 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} towards a street light which is {tex}5 \frac{1}{3} m{/tex} above the ground. At what rate is the tip of his shadow moving? At what rate is the length of the shadow changing when he is {tex}3 \frac{1}{3} m{/tex} from the base of the light?
question_html: A man, 2m tall, walks at the rate of {tex}1 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} towards a street light which is {tex}5 \frac{1}{3} m{/tex} above the ground. At what rate is the tip of his shadow moving? At what rate is the length of the shadow changing when he is {tex}3 \frac{1}{3} m{/tex} from the base of the light?
solution_text: Let AB be the street light post and CD be the height of man Let BC, X, CE = y Given {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{-5}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} From the figure, {tex}\Delta A B E{/tex} and {tex}\Delta D C E{/tex} are similar: {tex}\therefore\frac{A B}{D C}=\frac{B E}{C E}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{\frac{16}{3}}{\frac{2}{1}}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{16}{6}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{5} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{3}{5} \cdot\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right)=-1 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} Thus the length of the shadow is decreasing at the ratio of 1 m/s. For the tip of the shadow E, Let z = x + y Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get m {tex}\frac{d z}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}+\frac{d y}{d t}=-\left(\frac{5}{3}+1\right)=-\frac{8}{3}=-2 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} which is the rate at which shadow is moving Hence the tip of shadow is moving at the rate of {tex}2 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} towards the light sources.
solution_html:
Let AB be the street light post and CD be the height of man
Let BC, X, CE = y
Given {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{-5}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
From the figure, {tex}\Delta A B E{/tex} and {tex}\Delta D C E{/tex} are similar:
{tex}\therefore\frac{A B}{D C}=\frac{B E}{C E}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{\frac{16}{3}}{\frac{2}{1}}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{16}{6}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{5} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{3}{5} \cdot\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right)=-1 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
Thus the length of the shadow is decreasing at the ratio of 1 m/s.
For the tip of the shadow E,
Let z = x + y
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
m{tex}\frac{d z}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}+\frac{d y}{d t}=-\left(\frac{5}{3}+1\right)=-\frac{8}{3}=-2 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
which is the rate at which shadow is moving
Hence the tip of shadow is moving at the rate of {tex}2 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} towards the light sources.

question_text: A swimming pool is to be drained for cleaning. If L represents the number of litres of water in the pool t seconds after the pool has been plugged off to drain and L = 200 (10 – t) 2 . How fast is the water running out at the end of 5 seconds? What is the average rate at which the water flows out during the first 5 seconds?
question_html: A swimming pool is to be drained for cleaning. If L represents the number of litres of water in the pool t seconds after the pool has been plugged off to drain and L = 200 (10 – t)2. How fast is the water running out at the end of 5 seconds? What is the average rate at which the water flows out during the first 5 seconds?
solution_text: Let the rate at which the water is running out be given by {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} . Given L = 200 (10 - t) 2 Now differentiating the above equation with respect to t, we get {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left(200(10-\mathrm{t})^{2}\right)}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} Taking out the constant term, we get {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-200 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}\left((10-\mathrm{t})^{2}\right)}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} Applying the power rule of differentiating, we get {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-200.2(10-\mathrm{t}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}(10-\mathrm{t})}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-400(10-\mathrm{t}) \cdot(-1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 400 (10 - t) ...(i) Now we need to find how fast is the water running out at the end of 5 sec, so finding the value of equation (i) at t = 5, we get the answer {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = (400 (10 - t)) t = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = 400 (10 - 5) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = 400 (5) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}=2000 \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{s}}{/tex} ...(ii) Hence the rate at which the water is running out of the pool at the end of 5s is 2000 L/s Now to find the initial rate we will substitute t = 0 in equation (i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = (400 (10 - t) t = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = 400 (10 - 0) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = 4000 L/s ...(iii) So equation (ii) is the final rate and equation (iii) is the initial rate Hence the average rate during 5s is {tex}=\frac{\text { initial rate }+\text { final rate }}{2}{/tex} Substituting the corresponding values, we get {tex}=\frac{4000+2000}{2}{/tex} = 3000 L/s Hence , the average rate at which the water flows out during the first 5s is 3000 L/s .
solution_html:
Let the rate at which the water is running out be given by {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}.
Given L = 200 (10 - t)2
Now differentiating the above equation with respect to t, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left(200(10-\mathrm{t})^{2}\right)}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
Taking out the constant term, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-200 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}\left((10-\mathrm{t})^{2}\right)}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
Applying the power rule of differentiating, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-200.2(10-\mathrm{t}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}(10-\mathrm{t})}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-400(10-\mathrm{t}) \cdot(-1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 400 (10 - t) ...(i)
Now we need to find how fast is the water running out at the end of 5 sec, so finding the value of equation (i) at t = 5, we get the answer
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = (400 (10 - t))t = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = 400 (10 - 5)
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}{/tex} = 400 (5)
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=5}=2000 \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{s}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Hence the rate at which the water is running out of the pool at the end of 5s is 2000 L/s
Now to find the initial rate we will substitute t = 0 in equation (i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = (400 (10 - t)t = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = 400 (10 - 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=0}{/tex} = 4000 L/s ...(iii)
So equation (ii) is the final rate and equation (iii) is the initial rate
Hence the average rate during 5s is
{tex}=\frac{\text { initial rate }+\text { final rate }}{2}{/tex}
Substituting the corresponding values, we get
{tex}=\frac{4000+2000}{2}{/tex} = 3000 L/s
Hence ,  the average rate at which the water flows out during the first 5s is 3000 L/s .

question_text: The volume of a cube increases at a constant rate. Prove that the increase in its surface area varies inversely as the length of the side.
question_html: The volume of a cube increases at a constant rate. Prove that the increase in its surface area varies inversely as the length of the side.
solution_text: Let the side of a cube be x unit. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of cube (V) {tex} = {x^3}{/tex} On differentiating both side w.r.t. t, we get {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} [constant] {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{k}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) Also, surface area of cube, {tex}S = 6{x^2}{/tex} On differentiating w.r.t. t, we get {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = 12x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = 12x.\frac{k}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = \frac{{12k}}{{3x}} = 4\left( {\frac{k}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}}\alpha \frac{1}{x}{/tex} Hence, the surface area of the cube varies inversely as the length of the side.
solution_html:
Let the side of a cube be x unit.

{tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of cube (V) {tex} = {x^3}{/tex}

On differentiating both side w.r.t. t, we get

{tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} [constant]

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{k}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i)

Also, surface area of cube, {tex}S = 6{x^2}{/tex}

On differentiating w.r.t. t, we get

{tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = 12x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = 12x.\frac{k}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} [using Eq. (i)]

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}} = \frac{{12k}}{{3x}} = 4\left( {\frac{k}{x}} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}}\alpha \frac{1}{x}{/tex}

Hence, the surface area of the cube varies inversely as the length of the side.

question_text: x and y are the sides of two square such that {tex}y = x - {x^2}{/tex} Find the rate of change of the area of second square with respect to the area of first square.
question_html: x and y are the sides of two square such that {tex}y = x - {x^2}{/tex} Find the rate of change of the area of second square with respect to the area of first square.
solution_text: Since, x and y are the sides of two squares such that y = x - x 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the first square {tex}\left( {{A_1}} \right) = {x^2}{/tex} And area of the second square {tex}\left( {{A_2}} \right) = {y^2} = {\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{d{A_2}}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)^2} = 2\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} - 2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}\left( {1 - 2x} \right)2\left( {x = {x^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}= \frac{{d{A_1}}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2} = 2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{d{A_2}}}{{d{A_1}}} = \frac{{d{A_2}/dt}}{{d{A_1}/dt}} = \frac{{\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}.\left( {1 - 2x} \right)\left( {2x - 2{x^2}} \right)}}{{2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {1 - 2x} \right)2x\left( {1 - x} \right)}}{{2x}}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {1 - 2x} \right)\left( {1 - x} \right){/tex} {tex} = 1 - x - 2x + 2{x^2}{/tex} {tex} = 2{x^2} - 3x + 1{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, x and y are the sides of two squares such that y = x - x2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the first square {tex}\left( {{A_1}} \right) = {x^2}{/tex}
And area of the second square {tex}\left( {{A_2}} \right) = {y^2} = {\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{d{A_2}}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)^2} = 2\left( {x - {x^2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} - 2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}\left( {1 - 2x} \right)2\left( {x = {x^2}} \right){/tex} 
and {tex}= \frac{{d{A_1}}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2} = 2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{d{A_2}}}{{d{A_1}}} = \frac{{d{A_2}/dt}}{{d{A_1}/dt}} = \frac{{\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}.\left( {1 - 2x} \right)\left( {2x - 2{x^2}} \right)}}{{2x.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}}{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {1 - 2x} \right)2x\left( {1 - x} \right)}}{{2x}}{/tex} 
{tex} = \left( {1 - 2x} \right)\left( {1 - x} \right){/tex} 
{tex} = 1 - x - 2x + 2{x^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 2{x^2} - 3x + 1{/tex}

question_text: Find the condition that the curves 2x = y 2 and 2xy = k intersect orthogonally.
question_html: Find the condition that the curves 2x = y2 and 2xy = k intersect orthogonally.
solution_text: Given, equation of curves are 2x = y 2 ...(i) and 2xy = k ...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} For curve (i), {tex}2=2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1,} \text { or }\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and for curve (ii), {tex}2 y+2 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=0 \text { or }\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} Since curve intersect orthogonally, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)\left(-\frac{y}{x}\right)=-1{/tex} or x = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} from (i), y = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2}{/tex} Putting these values in equation (ii), we get k = {tex}\pm 2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} k 2 = 8
solution_html:
Given, equation of curves are 2x = y2 ...(i)
and 2xy = k ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For curve (i),
 {tex}2=2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1,} \text { or }\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex}
and for curve (ii),
 {tex}2 y+2 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=0 \text { or }\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Since curve intersect orthogonally, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)\left(-\frac{y}{x}\right)=-1{/tex}
or x = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} from (i), y = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Putting these values in equation (ii), we get
k = {tex}\pm 2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k2 = 8

question_text: Prove that the curves xy = 4 and x 2 + y 2 = 8 touch each other.
question_html: Prove that the curves xy = 4 and x2 + y2 = 8 touch each other.
solution_text: Given equation of curves are xy = 4 ...(i) and x 2 + y 2 = 8 ...(ii) Let's first find the point of intersection of curves From equation (i), {tex}y=\frac{4}{x}{/tex} Putting this in equation (ii), we get {tex}x^{2}+\frac{16}{x^{2}}=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 - 8x 2 + 16 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - 4) 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm2{/tex} (using (i)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points of intersection are (2, 2) and (-2, -2) For curve (i), {tex}y+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_1=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} AT point (2, 2) and (-2, -2), {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}{/tex} = -1 For curve (ii), 2x + 2y {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} At point (2, 2) and (-2, -2), {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = -1 Thus two curves touch each other at their points of intersection.
solution_html:
Given equation of curves are
xy = 4 ...(i)
and x2 + y2 = 8 ...(ii)
Let's first find the point of intersection of curves
From equation (i), {tex}y=\frac{4}{x}{/tex}
Putting this in equation (ii), we get
{tex}x^{2}+\frac{16}{x^{2}}=8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4 - 8x2 + 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - 4)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm2{/tex} (using (i))
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Points of intersection are (2, 2) and (-2, -2)
For curve (i), {tex}y+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=-\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
AT point (2, 2) and (-2, -2), {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}{/tex} = -1
For curve (ii), 2x + 2y {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
At point (2, 2) and (-2, -2), {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = -1
Thus two curves touch each other at their points of intersection.

question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the point on the curve {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = 4{/tex} at which tangent is equally inclined to the axes.
question_html: Find the co-ordinates of the point on the curve {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = 4{/tex} at which tangent is equally inclined to the axes.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = 4{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow {x^{1/2}} + {y^{1/2}} = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}.\frac{1}{{{x^{1.2}}}} + \frac{1}{2}.\frac{1}{{{y^{1/.2}}}}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{1}{2}.{x^{ - 1/2}}2.{y^{1/2}}{/tex} {tex}= - \sqrt {\frac{y}{x}}{/tex} Since, tangent is equally inclined to the axes. {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \pm 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \sqrt {\frac{y}{x}} = \pm 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = 1 \Rightarrow y = x{/tex} From Eq. (i), {tex}\sqrt y + \sqrt y = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\sqrt y = 4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4y = 16{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = 4{/tex} and x = 4 When y = 4 and x = 4 So, the required coordinates are (4, 4).
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = 4{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}\Rightarrow {x^{1/2}} + {y^{1/2}} = 4{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}.\frac{1}{{{x^{1.2}}}} + \frac{1}{2}.\frac{1}{{{y^{1/.2}}}}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{1}{2}.{x^{ - 1/2}}2.{y^{1/2}}{/tex}

{tex}= - \sqrt {\frac{y}{x}}{/tex}

Since, tangent is equally inclined to the axes.

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \pm 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow - \sqrt {\frac{y}{x}} = \pm 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = 1 \Rightarrow y = x{/tex}

From Eq. (i), {tex}\sqrt y + \sqrt y = 4{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2\sqrt y = 4{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 4y = 16{/tex}

{tex}\therefore y = 4{/tex} and x = 4

When y = 4 and x = 4

So, the required coordinates are (4, 4).

question_text: Find the angle of intersection of the curves y = 4 - x 2 and y = x 2 .
question_html: Find the angle of intersection of the curves y = 4 - x2 and y = x2.
solution_text: We have, y = 4 - x 2 ...(i) and y = x 2 ...(ii) Solving these equations, we get x 2 = 4 - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\pm \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = x 2 = 2 So, the points of intersection are {tex}(\sqrt{2}, 2){/tex} and {tex}(-\sqrt{2}, 2){/tex} Now for first curve, {tex}m_{1}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}{/tex} = -2x For second curve, {tex}m_{2}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = 2x At point {tex}(\sqrt{2}, 2), m_{1}=-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}m_{2}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} If angle between curves is {tex}'\theta',{/tex} then {tex}\therefore \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{-2 \sqrt{2}-2 \sqrt{2}}{1-2 \sqrt{2} \cdot 2 \sqrt{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{-4 \sqrt{2}}{-7}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta=\tan ^{-1} \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{7}{/tex} For point {tex}(-\sqrt{2}, 2), m_{1}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}m_{2}=-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For this point also {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1} \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
y = 4 - x2 ...(i)
and y = x2 ...(ii)
Solving these equations, we get
x2 = 4 - x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = x2 = 2
So, the points of intersection are {tex}(\sqrt{2}, 2){/tex} and {tex}(-\sqrt{2}, 2){/tex}
Now for first curve, {tex}m_{1}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}{/tex} = -2x
For second curve, {tex}m_{2}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}{/tex} = 2x
At point {tex}(\sqrt{2}, 2), m_{1}=-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}m_{2}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
If angle between curves is {tex}'\theta',{/tex} then
{tex}\therefore \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{-2 \sqrt{2}-2 \sqrt{2}}{1-2 \sqrt{2} \cdot 2 \sqrt{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{-4 \sqrt{2}}{-7}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta=\tan ^{-1} \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{7}{/tex}
For point {tex}(-\sqrt{2}, 2), m_{1}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}m_{2}=-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For this point also {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1} \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{7}{/tex}

question_text: Prove that the curves {tex}{y^2} = 4x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + 1 = 0{/tex} touch each other at the point (1, 2).
question_html: Prove that the curves {tex}{y^2} = 4x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + 1 = 0{/tex} touch each other at the point (1, 2).
solution_text: We have, {tex}{y^2} = 4x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + 1 = 0{/tex} Since, both the curves touch each other at (1, 2) i.e., curves are passing through (1, 2). {tex}\therefore 2y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 4{/tex} and {tex}2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{4}{{2y}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{6 - 2y}}{{2y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {1,2} \right)}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1{/tex} and {tex}{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {1,2} \right)}} = \frac{{6 - 2.1}}{{2.2}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {m_1} = 1{/tex} and {tex}{m_2} = 1{/tex} Thus, we see that slope of both the curves are equal to each other i.e., {tex}{m_1} = {m_2} = 1{/tex} at the point (1, 2). Hence, both the curves touch each other.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{y^2} = 4x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + 1 = 0{/tex} 

Since, both the curves touch each other at (1, 2) i.e., curves are passing through (1, 2).

{tex}\therefore 2y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 4{/tex}

and {tex}2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 6{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{4}{{2y}}{/tex}

and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{6 - 2y}}{{2y}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {1,2} \right)}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1{/tex}

and {tex}{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {1,2} \right)}} = \frac{{6 - 2.1}}{{2.2}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {m_1} = 1{/tex} and {tex}{m_2} = 1{/tex}

Thus, we see that slope of both the curves are equal to each other i.e., {tex}{m_1} = {m_2} = 1{/tex} at the point (1, 2).

Hence, both the curves touch each other.

question_text: Find the equation of the normal lines to the curve 3x 2 - y 2 = 8 which are parallel to the line x + 3y = 4.
question_html: Find the equation of the normal lines to the curve 3x2 - y2 = 8 which are parallel to the line x + 3y = 4.
solution_text: Given equation of the curve is 3x 2 - y 2 = 8 ...(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}6 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x}{y},{/tex} which is slope of tangent at any point on the curve {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of normal at any point on the curve is {tex}-\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{-y}{3 x}{/tex} Given that the normal is parallel to the line x + 3y = 4 {tex}\therefore -\frac{y}{3 x}=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ...[Slope of Tangent] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), we get 3x 2 - x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\pm 2{/tex} For x = 2, y = 2 [using (iii)] and for x = -2, y = -2 [using (iii)] Thus, the points on the curve at which normal to the curve are parallel to the line x + 3y = 4 are (2, 2) and (-2, -2) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equations of normal are y - 2 = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} (x - 2) and y + 2 = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} (x + 2) or 3y + x = 8 and 3y + x = -8
solution_html:
Given equation of the curve is
3x2 - y2 = 8 ...(i)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}6 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x}{y},{/tex} which is slope of tangent at any point on the curve
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of normal at any point on the curve is {tex}-\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{-y}{3 x}{/tex}
Given that the normal is parallel to the line x + 3y = 4
{tex}\therefore -\frac{y}{3 x}=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}   ...[Slope of Tangent]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
3x2 - x2 = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\pm 2{/tex}
For x = 2, y = 2 [using (iii)]
and for x = -2, y = -2 [using (iii)]
Thus, the points on the curve at which normal to the curve are parallel to the line x + 3y = 4 are (2, 2) and (-2, -2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equations of normal are
y - 2 = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} (x - 2) and y + 2 = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} (x + 2)
or 3y + x = 8 and 3y + x = -8

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: At what points on the curve x 2 + y 2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0, the tangents are parallel to the y-axis?
question_html: At what points on the curve x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0, the tangents are parallel to the y-axis?
solution_text: Given, equation of curve is x 2 + y 2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0 ...(i) Differentiating w.r.t. s, we get, 2x + 2y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2 - 4 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x}{y-2}{/tex} Since, the tangents are parallel to the y-axis, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x}{y-2} \rightarrow \infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 Putting this value of y in equation (i), we get x 2 + 4 - 2x - 8 + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - 2x - 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1, x = 3 So, the required points are (-1, 2) and (3, 2)
solution_html:
Given, equation of curve is
x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0 ...(i)
Differentiating w.r.t. s, we get,
2x + 2y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2 - 4 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x}{y-2}{/tex}
Since, the tangents are parallel to the y-axis, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x}{y-2} \rightarrow \infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2
Putting this value of y in equation (i), we get
x2 + 4 - 2x - 8 + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1, x = 3
So, the required points are (-1, 2) and (3, 2)

question_text: Show that the line {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 1, touches the curve {tex}y=b \cdot e^{\frac{-x}{a}}{/tex} at the point where the curve intersects the axis of y.
question_html: Show that the line {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 1, touches the curve {tex}y=b \cdot e^{\frac{-x}{a}}{/tex} at the point where the curve intersects the axis of y.
solution_text: Given equation of the curve, {tex}y=b \cdot e^{\frac{-x}{a}}{/tex} ...(i) It meets y-axis, {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = b e 0 = b {tex}\therefore{/tex} point where it meets y-axis is (0, b) Differentiating (i), w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} e^{-x / a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0, b)}=-\frac{b}{a} e^{-0}=-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent at point (0, b) is y - b = {tex}-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} (x - 0) {tex}\therefore{/tex} ay - ab = -bx or {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 1 Hence, the line touches the curve at the point, where the curve intersects the axis of y.
solution_html:
Given equation of the curve, {tex}y=b \cdot e^{\frac{-x}{a}}{/tex} ...(i)
It meets y-axis,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = b e0 = b
{tex}\therefore{/tex} point where it meets y-axis is (0, b)
Differentiating (i), w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} e^{-x / a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0, b)}=-\frac{b}{a} e^{-0}=-\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent at point (0, b) is
y - b = {tex}-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} (x - 0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ay - ab = -bx
or {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 1
Hence, the line touches the curve at the point, where the curve intersects the axis of y.

question_text: Show that f(x) = 2x + cot -1 x + log {tex}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x){/tex} is increasing in R .
question_html: Show that f(x) = 2x + cot-1x + log {tex}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x){/tex} is increasing in R.
solution_text: We have, f(x) = 2x + cot -1 x + log {tex}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x){/tex} {tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}-1\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x} \cdot \frac{x-\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex} Now, 1 + x 2 , {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}} \geq 1{/tex} for all real x, {tex}\therefore 2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}>0{/tex} for all real x, {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) > 0 for all real x Thus f(x) is increasing of R.
solution_html:
We have, f(x) = 2x + cot-1x + log {tex}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}-1\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x} \cdot \frac{x-\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex}
Now, 1 + x2, {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}} \geq 1{/tex} for all real x,
{tex}\therefore 2-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}>0{/tex} for all real x,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) > 0 for all real x
Thus f(x) is increasing of R.

question_text: Show that for a ≥ 1, f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sinx - cosx – 2ax + b is decreasing in R .
question_html: Show that for a ≥ 1, f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sinx - cosx – 2ax + b is decreasing in R.
solution_text: We have f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sinx - cosx – 2ax + b {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cosx + sinx - 2a {tex}=2\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos x+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \sin x\right]-2 a{/tex} {tex}=2\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot \cos x+\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot \sin x\right]-2 a=2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)-2 a{/tex} If f(x) is decreasing of R, {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0 for all real x {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)-2 a \leq 0{/tex} for all real x {tex}\Rightarrow a \geq \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right){/tex} for all real x {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a {tex}\geq{/tex} max. value of cos {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a {tex}\geq{/tex} 1
solution_html:
We have f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} sinx - cosx – 2ax + b
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cosx + sinx - 2a
{tex}=2\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos x+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \sin x\right]-2 a{/tex}
{tex}=2\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot \cos x+\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot \sin x\right]-2 a=2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)-2 a{/tex}
If f(x) is decreasing of R,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0 for all real x
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)-2 a \leq 0{/tex} for all real x
{tex}\Rightarrow a \geq \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right){/tex} for all real x
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a {tex}\geq{/tex} max. value of cos {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a {tex}\geq{/tex} 1

question_text: Show that {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
question_html: Show that {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
solution_text: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} For critical points let f '(x)=0 {tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} = 0{/tex} cosx (1 - tanx) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx = 0, or 1 - tanx = 0 since {tex}{ \tan x < 1~~\forall \ x\in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} {/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) < 0~~\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} Hence f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex}
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
For critical points let  f '(x)=0
{tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} = 0{/tex}
cosx (1 - tanx) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx = 0, or 1 - tanx = 0 
since {tex}{ \tan x < 1~~\forall \ x\in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} {/tex}
{tex}f'\left( x \right) < 0~~\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
Hence f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}

question_text: At what point, the slope of the curve y = -x 3 + 3x 2 + 9x - 27 is maximum? Also, find the maximum slope.
question_html: At what point, the slope of the curve y = -x3 + 3x2 + 9x - 27 is maximum? Also, find the maximum slope.
solution_text: We have, {tex}y = - {x^3} + 3{x^2} + 9x - 27{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 3{x^2} + 6x + 9 = 0{/tex} =Slope of tangent to the curve Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 6x + 6{/tex} For {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) = 0,{/tex} -6x + 6 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{ - 6}}{{ - 6}} = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right) = - 6 < 0{/tex} So, the slope of tangent to the curve is maximum, when x = 1 For x = 1, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {x = 1} \right)}} = - {3.1^2} + 6.1 + 9 = 12,{/tex} Which is maximum slope. Also, for x = 1, {tex}y = - {1^3} + {3.1^2} + 9.1 - 27{/tex} = -1 + 3 + 9 - 27 = -16 So, the required point is (1, - 16)
solution_html:
We have, {tex}y = - {x^3} + 3{x^2} + 9x - 27{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 3{x^2} + 6x + 9 = 0{/tex} =Slope of tangent to the curve
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 6x + 6{/tex}
For {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) = 0,{/tex}
-6x + 6 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{ - 6}}{{ - 6}} = 1{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right) = - 6 < 0{/tex}
So, the slope of tangent to the curve is maximum, when x = 1
For x = 1, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {x = 1} \right)}} = - {3.1^2} + 6.1 + 9 = 12,{/tex}
Which is maximum slope.
Also, for x = 1, {tex}y = - {1^3} + {3.1^2} + 9.1 - 27{/tex}
= -1 + 3 + 9 - 27
= -16
So, the required point is (1, - 16)

question_text: Prove that {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin x + \sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex} has maximum value at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} .
question_html: Prove that {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin x + \sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex} has maximum value at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin x + \sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex} {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = \cos x + \sqrt 3 \left( { - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex} = \cos x - \sqrt 3 \sin x{/tex} For {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0,\cos x = \sqrt 3 \sin x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \tan x = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \tan \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} Again, differentiating {tex}f'\left( x \right){/tex} we get {tex}f''\left( x \right) = - \sin x-\sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},f''\left( x \right) = - \sin \frac{\pi }{6} - \sqrt 3 \cos \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2} - \sqrt 3 .\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{3}{2} = - 2 < 0{/tex} Hence, at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},f\left( x \right){/tex} has maximum value and maximum value is f( {tex}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6}{/tex} ) {tex} = \sin \frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6} + \sqrt 3 \cos \frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6}=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{3}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}){/tex} . {tex}=\frac{1}{2}+1=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin x + \sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex}

{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = \cos x + \sqrt 3 \left( { - \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \cos x - \sqrt 3 \sin x{/tex}

For {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0,\cos x = \sqrt 3 \sin x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \tan x = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \tan \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

Again, differentiating {tex}f'\left( x \right){/tex}we get

{tex}f''\left( x \right) = - \sin x-\sqrt 3 \cos x{/tex}

At {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},f''\left( x \right) = - \sin \frac{\pi }{6} - \sqrt 3 \cos \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{2} - \sqrt 3 .\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex}

{tex} = - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{3}{2} = - 2 < 0{/tex}

Hence, at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},f\left( x \right){/tex} has maximum value and maximum value is f({tex}\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6}{/tex}){tex} = \sin \frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6} + \sqrt 3 \cos \frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6}=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{3}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}){/tex}.

 {tex}=\frac{1}{2}+1=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_text: If the sum of lengths of the hypotenuse and a side of a right angled triangle is given, show that the area of the triangle is maximum, when the angle between them is {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }.{/tex}
question_html: If the sum of lengths of the hypotenuse and a side of a right angled triangle is given, show that the area of the triangle is maximum, when the angle between them is  {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }.{/tex}
solution_text: Let us consider a right-angled triangle with base = x and hypotenuse = y. Let x + y = k, where k is a constant. Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between the base and the hypotenuse. Let A be the area of the triangle, then {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times Q R \times P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^{ 2 } \left( y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } \left[ ( k - x ) ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right] } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = k - x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 2 k x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 A \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } x - 6 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 A }{/tex} Now, for maxima or minima Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow \left( k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} Differentiating (ii) w.r.t x,we get, {tex}2 \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 A \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } - 12 k x } { 4 }{/tex} .......(iii) Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - k ^ { 2 } } { 4 A } < 0{/tex} Thus, A is maximum when {tex}x = \left( \frac { k } { 3 } \right){/tex} Now, {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \left( k - \frac { k } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 2 k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x } { y } = \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { k / 3 } { 2 k / 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} Hence, the area of triangle is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let us consider a right-angled triangle with base = x and hypotenuse = y. Let x + y = k, where k is a constant. Let {tex}\theta{/tex}  be the angle between the base and the hypotenuse. Let A be the area of the triangle, then

{tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times Q R \times P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^{ 2 } \left( y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } \left[ ( k - x ) ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right] } { 4 }{/tex}{tex}[ \because y = k - x ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 2 k x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}.....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 A \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } x - 6 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 A }{/tex}
Now, for maxima or minima
Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow \left( k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} 
Differentiating (ii) w.r.t x,we get,
 {tex}2 \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 A \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } - 12 k x } { 4 }{/tex}.......(iii)
Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (iii), we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - k ^ { 2 } } { 4 A } < 0{/tex}
Thus, A is maximum when {tex}x = \left( \frac { k } { 3 } \right){/tex}
Now, {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \left( k - \frac { k } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 2 k } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x } { y } = \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { k / 3 } { 2 k / 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
Hence, the area of triangle is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.

question_text: Find the points of local maxima, local minima and the points of inflection of the function f(x) = x 5 - 5x 4 + 5x 3 - 1. Also, find the corresponding local maximum and local minimum values.
question_html: Find the points of local maxima, local minima and the points of inflection of the function f(x) = x5 - 5x4 + 5x3 - 1. Also, find the corresponding local maximum and local minimum values.
solution_text: Given that, f(x) = x 5 - 5x 4 + 5x 3 - 1 On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = 5x 4 - 20x 3 + 15x 2 For maxima or minima f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x 4 - 20x 3 + 15x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x 2 (x 2 - 4x + 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x 2 (x 2 - 3x - x + 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x 2 [x(x - 3) - 1 (x - 3)] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x 2 [(x-3) (x - 3)] = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0, 1 , 3 Sign scheme for {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 5{x^2}\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right){/tex} So, y has maximum value at x = 1 and minimum value at x = 3. At x = 0, y has neither maximum nor minimum value, so x = 0 is the point of inflection Maximum value of y = 1 - 5 + 5 -1 = 0 (at x =1) and Minimum value of y = 3 5 - 5(3) 4 + 5(3) 3 - 1 = 243 - 405 + 135 - 1 = -28 (at x = 3)
solution_html:
Given that, f(x) = x5 - 5x4 + 5x3 - 1 
On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = 5x4 - 20x3 + 15x2 
For maxima or minima f'(x) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x4 - 20x3 + 15x2 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x2 (x2 - 4x + 3) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x2 (x2 - 3x - x + 3) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x2 [x(x - 3) - 1 (x - 3)] = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x2 [(x-3) (x - 3)] = 0 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0, 1 , 3 
Sign  scheme for {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 5{x^2}\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right){/tex} 

So, y has maximum value at x = 1 and minimum value at x = 3.
At x = 0, y has neither maximum nor minimum value, so  x = 0 is the point of inflection
Maximum value of y = 1 - 5 + 5 -1 = 0 (at x =1) and Minimum value of y = 35 - 5(3)4 + 5(3)3 - 1 = 243 - 405 + 135 - 1 = -28 (at x = 3)

question_text: A telephone company in a town has 500 subscribers on its list and collects fixed charges of ₹ 300/- per subscriber per year. The company proposes to increase the annual subscription and it is believed that for every increase of ₹ 1/- one subscriber will discontinue the service. Find what increase will bring maximum profit?
question_html: A telephone company in a town has 500 subscribers on its list and collects fixed charges of ₹ 300/- per subscriber per year. The company proposes to increase the annual subscription and it is believed that for every increase of ₹ 1/- one subscriber will discontinue the service. Find what increase will bring maximum profit?
solution_text: Consider that company increases the annual subscription by ₹ x So, x subscribes will discontinue the service. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total revenue of company after the increment is given by R(x) = (500 - x) (300 + x) = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 + 500x - 300x - x 2 = -x 2 + 200x + 150000 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get R'(x) = -2x + 200 Now, R'(x) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow 2x = 200 \Rightarrow x = 100{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R"(x) = -2 < 0 So, R(x) is maximum when x = 100 Hence, the company should increase the subscription fee by ₹ 100, so that it has maximum profit.
solution_html:
Consider that company increases the annual subscription by ₹ x
So, x subscribes will discontinue the service.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total revenue of company after the increment is given by
R(x) = (500 - x) (300 + x)
= 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 + 500x - 300x - x2
= -x2 + 200x + 150000
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
R'(x) = -2x + 200
Now, R'(x) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow 2x = 200 \Rightarrow x = 100{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R"(x) = -2 < 0
So, R(x) is maximum when x = 100
Hence, the company should increase the subscription fee by ₹ 100, so that it has maximum profit.

question_text: If the straight line {tex}x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p{/tex} touches the curve {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1, then prove that {tex}a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p^{2}{/tex}
question_html: If the straight line {tex}x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p{/tex} touches the curve {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1, then prove that {tex}a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p^{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Given, line is {tex}x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p{/tex} ...(i) and curve is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} or b 2 x 2 + a 2 y 2 = a 2 b 2 ...(ii) Let line and curve touches each other at point P(x 1 , y 1 ) {tex}\therefore x_{1} \cos \alpha+y_{1} \sin \alpha=p{/tex} ...(iii) and {tex}b^{2} x_{1}^{2}+a^{2} y_{1}^{2}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex} ...(iv) Also slope of line (i) is {tex}-\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha}{/tex} Differentiating (ii), w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2 b^{2} x+2 a^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b^{2} x}{a^{2} y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)}=-\frac{b^{2} x_{1}}{a^{2} y_{1}}{/tex} According to the question {tex}-\frac{b^{2} x_{1}}{a^{2} y_{1}}=-\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}}{a^{2} \cos \alpha}=\frac{y_{1}}{b^{2} \sin \alpha}=\lambda{/tex} (say) {tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=\lambda a^{2} \cos \alpha{/tex} and {tex}y_{1}=\lambda b^{2} \sin \alpha{/tex} Putting these values in (iii) {tex}\lambda a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+\lambda b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{p}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=\frac{p a^{2} \cos \alpha}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} and {tex}y_{1}=\frac{p b^{2} \sin \alpha}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} Putting these values in (iv), we get {tex}\frac{b^{2} p^{2} a^{4} \cos ^{2} \alpha}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}+\frac{a^{2} p^{2} b^{4} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2} b^{2} p^{2}\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+\dot{b}^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, line is {tex}x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p{/tex} ...(i)
and curve is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
or b2x2 + a2y2 = a2b2 ...(ii)
Let line and curve touches each other at point P(x1, y1)
{tex}\therefore x_{1} \cos \alpha+y_{1} \sin \alpha=p{/tex} ...(iii)
and {tex}b^{2} x_{1}^{2}+a^{2} y_{1}^{2}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex} ...(iv)
Also slope of line (i) is {tex}-\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha}{/tex}
Differentiating (ii), w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}2 b^{2} x+2 a^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b^{2} x}{a^{2} y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)}=-\frac{b^{2} x_{1}}{a^{2} y_{1}}{/tex}
According to the question {tex}-\frac{b^{2} x_{1}}{a^{2} y_{1}}=-\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}}{a^{2} \cos \alpha}=\frac{y_{1}}{b^{2} \sin \alpha}=\lambda{/tex} (say)
{tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=\lambda a^{2} \cos \alpha{/tex} and {tex}y_{1}=\lambda b^{2} \sin \alpha{/tex}
Putting these values in (iii)
{tex}\lambda a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+\lambda b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{p}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=\frac{p a^{2} \cos \alpha}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} and {tex}y_{1}=\frac{p b^{2} \sin \alpha}{a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
Putting these values in (iv), we get
{tex}\frac{b^{2} p^{2} a^{4} \cos ^{2} \alpha}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}+\frac{a^{2} p^{2} b^{4} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2} b^{2} p^{2}\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+\dot{b}^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha\right)^{2}}=a^{2} b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha=p^{2}{/tex}

question_text: An open box with a square base is to be made out of a given quantity of cardboard of area c 2 . Show that the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cubic units.
question_html: An open box with a square base is to be made out of a given quantity of cardboard of area c2. Show that the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cubic units.
solution_text: Let the dimensions of the box be x and y. Also, let V denote its volume and S denotes its total surface area. Now, S = x 2 + 4xy Given, x 2 + 4xy = C 2 {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x }{/tex} ...(i) Also, volume of the box is given by V = x 2 y {tex} \Rightarrow V = x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x } \right){/tex} [From Eq.(i)] {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac{dV}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C 2 = 3x 2 {tex}\therefore x = C / \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d V } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 6 x } { 4 } = \frac { - 3 x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left. \frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right| _ { a t x = C / \sqrt { 3 } } < 0{/tex} This implies V is maximum. Now, maximum volume at x = {tex} \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} is {tex}V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \cdot C ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] \left[ \text { put } x = \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } - \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { 3 C ^ { 3 } -C^3} { 3 \sqrt3}\right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \times \frac { 2 C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } = \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Hence, the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cu units.
solution_html:
Let the dimensions of the box be x and y. Also, let V denote its volume and S denotes its total surface area.
Now, S = x2 + 4xy
Given, x2 + 4xy = C2
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x }{/tex}    ...(i)
Also, volume of the box is given by
V = x2y
{tex} \Rightarrow V = x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x } \right){/tex}[From Eq.(i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac{dV}{dx}{/tex} = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C2 = 3x2
{tex}\therefore x = C / \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d V } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 6 x } { 4 } = \frac { - 3 x } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left. \frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right| _ { a t x = C / \sqrt { 3 } } < 0{/tex}
This implies V is maximum.
Now, maximum volume at x ={tex} \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} is
{tex}V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \cdot C ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] \left[ \text { put } x = \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } - \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { 3 C ^ { 3 } -C^3} { 3 \sqrt3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \times \frac { 2 C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } = \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Hence, the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cu units.

question_text: Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.
question_html: Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.
solution_text: Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i) Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y. Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)] {tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum. So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder, {tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} {tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm
{tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i)
Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y.
Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)]
{tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} 
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} 
Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} 
At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum.
So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder,
{tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} 
{tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}

question_text: If the sum of the surface areas of cube and a sphere is constant, what is the ratio of an edge of the cube to the diameter of the sphere, when the sum of their volumes is minimum?
question_html: If the sum of the surface areas of cube and a sphere is constant, what is the ratio of an edge of the cube to the diameter of the sphere, when the sum of their volumes is minimum?
solution_text: Let length of one edge of cube be x units and radius of sphere be r units. Now surface area of cube = 6x 2 And surface area of sphere = {tex}4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} According to the question 6x 2 + {tex}4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} = k, where k is constant {tex}\Rightarrow x=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} Now,volume of cube = x 3 and volume of sphere = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} The sum of volume of the cube and volume of the sphere is {tex}S=x^{3}+\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{3 / 2}+\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d r}=-2 \pi r\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}+4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}=-2 \pi r\left[\left\{\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right\}^{1 / 2}-2 r\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d S}{d r}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 2 r=\left(\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex} (as r {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) {tex}\Rightarrow 4 r^{2}=\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 24 r^{2}=k-4 \pi^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r^{2}[24+4 \pi]=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r=\sqrt{\frac{k}{24+4 \pi}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{k}{6+\pi}}{/tex} Clearly, this is point of minima For {tex}r=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{k}{6+\pi}}, x=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{k}{(6+\pi)}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{(6+\pi) k-\pi k}{6(6+\pi)}\right]^{1 / 2}=\left[\frac{k}{6+\pi}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = 2r Thus, the sum of their volume is minimum when x = 2r Hence, the ratio of an edge of cube to the diameter of the sphere is 1 : 1
solution_html:
Let length of one edge of cube be x units and radius of sphere be r units.
Now surface area of cube = 6x2
And surface area of sphere = {tex}4 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
According to the question
6x2 + {tex}4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} = k, where k is constant
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
Now,volume of cube = x3
and volume of sphere = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}
The sum of volume of the cube and volume of the sphere is
{tex}S=x^{3}+\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}{tex}=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{3 / 2}+\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d r}=-2 \pi r\left[\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}+4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}=-2 \pi r\left[\left\{\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right\}^{1 / 2}-2 r\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d S}{d r}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 r=\left(\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex} (as r {tex}\neq{/tex} 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 r^{2}=\frac{k-4 \pi r^{2}}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 24 r^{2}=k-4 \pi^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r^{2}[24+4 \pi]=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\sqrt{\frac{k}{24+4 \pi}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{k}{6+\pi}}{/tex}
Clearly, this is point of minima
For {tex}r=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{k}{6+\pi}}, x=\left[\frac{k-4 \pi \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{k}{(6+\pi)}}{6}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{(6+\pi) k-\pi k}{6(6+\pi)}\right]^{1 / 2}=\left[\frac{k}{6+\pi}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = 2r
Thus, the sum of their volume is minimum when x = 2r
Hence, the ratio of an edge of cube to the diameter of the sphere is 1 : 1

Also, {tex}\angle C = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} angle made in semi-circle is 90°] In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, we have (AB) 2 =(AC) 2 + (BC) 2 (2r) 2 = (x) 2 + (y) 2 4r 2 = x 2 + y 2 ...(I) We know that, Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, {tex}( A ) = _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } x \cdot y{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} Let A 2 =S Then, {tex}S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } \left( 4 r ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 4 x ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2} - x ^4 \right){/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) = 0{/tex} 8r 2 x = 4x 3 8r 2 = 4x 2 x 2 = 2r 2 x = {tex}\sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get, {tex}y ^ { 2 } = 4 r ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { 2 } = 2 r ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} Here, x = y so triangle is an isosceles. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } - 12 x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 r ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} m At {tex}x = \sqrt 2 r,\frac{{{d^2}S}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2{r^2} - 3(2{r^2})=-4r^2 < 0 {/tex} Therefore, Area of the triangle is maximum when it is an isosceles triangle.
solution_html:
Let AC = x, BC = y and r be the radius of circle.
Also, {tex}\angle C = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex} angle made in semi-circle is 90°]
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, we have
(AB)2 =(AC)2 + (BC)2
(2r)2 = (x)2 + (y)2
4r2 = x2 + y2   ...(I)

We know that,
Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, {tex}( A ) = _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } x \cdot y{/tex}
On squaring both sides, we get
{tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Let  A2=S
Then, {tex}S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } \left( 4 r ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}[from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 4 x ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2} - x ^4 \right){/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) = 0{/tex}
8r2x = 4x3
8r2 = 4x2
x2 = 2r2
x = {tex}\sqrt { 2 } r{/tex}
From Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}y ^ { 2 } = 4 r ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { 2 } = 2 r ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex}
Here, x = y so triangle is an isosceles.
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } - 12 x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2 r ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}m
At {tex}x = \sqrt 2 r,\frac{{{d^2}S}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2{r^2} - 3(2{r^2})=-4r^2 < 0 {/tex} 
Therefore, Area of the triangle is maximum when it is an isosceles triangle.
