question_text: A metal box with a square base and vertical sides is to contain 1024 cm 3 . The material for the top and bottom costs ₹ 5/cm 2 and the material for the sides costs ₹ 2.50/cm 2 . Find the least cost of the box.
question_html: A metal box with a square base and vertical sides is to contain 1024 cm3. The material for the top and bottom costs ₹ 5/cm2 and the material for the sides costs ₹ 2.50/cm2. Find the least cost of the box.
solution_text: Since, volume of the box = 1024 cm 3 Let length of the side of square base be x cm and height of the box be y cm. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the box (V) {tex}= {x^2}.y = 1024{/tex} Since, {tex}{x^2}y = 1024 \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} Let C denotes the cost of the box. {tex}\therefore C = 2{x^2} \times 5 + 4xy \times 2.50{/tex} {tex}= 10{x^2} + 10xy = 10x\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} {tex}= 10x\left( {x + \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{10x}}{{{x^2}}}\left( {{x^3} + 1024} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C = 10{x^2} + \frac{{1024}}{x}{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 20x - 10240{\left( { x} \right)^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= 20x - \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex} \frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 20x = \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 20{x^3} = 10240{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} = 512 = {8^3} \Rightarrow x = 8{/tex} Again, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}} = 20 - 10240\left( { - 2} \right).\frac{1}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} {tex}= 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 8}} = 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{512}} = 60 > 0{/tex} For x = 8, cost is minimum and the corresponding least cost of the box {tex}C\left( 8 \right) = {10.8^2} + \frac{{10240}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Least cost = ₹ 1920
solution_html:
Since, volume of the box = 1024 cm3 
Let length of the side of square base be x cm and height of the box be y cm.

{tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the box (V) {tex}= {x^2}.y = 1024{/tex} 
Since, {tex}{x^2}y = 1024 \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} 
Let C denotes the cost of the box.
{tex}\therefore C = 2{x^2} \times 5 + 4xy \times 2.50{/tex} 
{tex}= 10{x^2} + 10xy = 10x\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= 10x\left( {x + \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{10x}}{{{x^2}}}\left( {{x^3} + 1024} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow C = 10{x^2} + \frac{{1024}}{x}{/tex} ...(i)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 20x - 10240{\left( { x} \right)^{ - 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}= 20x - \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}...(ii)
Now, {tex} \frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 20x = \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow 20{x^3} = 10240{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} = 512 = {8^3} \Rightarrow x = 8{/tex} 
Again, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}} = 20 - 10240\left( { - 2} \right).\frac{1}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} 
{tex}= 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} 
{tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 8}} = 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{512}} = 60 > 0{/tex} 
For x = 8, cost is minimum and the corresponding least cost of the box
{tex}C\left( 8 \right) = {10.8^2} + \frac{{10240}}{8}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Least cost = ₹ 1920

question_text: The sum of the surface areas of a cuboid with sides x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} and a sphere is given to be constant. Prove that the sum of their volumes is minimum, if x is equal of three times the radius of sphere. Also, find the minimum value of the sum of their volumes.
question_html: The sum of the surface areas of a cuboid with sides x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} and a sphere is given to be constant. Prove that the sum of their volumes is minimum, if  x is equal of three times the radius of sphere. Also, find the minimum value of the sum of their volumes.
solution_text: Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} . {tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given] {tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} ......(i) Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} \sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=3r [using Eq. (i)] Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex} w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex} {tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex} Now, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex} Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere. Hence proved. Now, on putting {tex} r = \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}V _ { \mathrm { min } } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 } \left( \frac { x } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 81 } x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 2 \pi } { 27 } + 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 44 } { 189 } + 1 \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 233 } { 189 } \right) = \frac { 466 } { 567 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and  {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex}.
{tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given]
{tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}......(i)
Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}....(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}\sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=3r
[using Eq. (i)]
Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex}w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex}
Now, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex}
Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere.
Hence proved.
Now, on putting {tex} r = \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (ii), we get 
{tex}V _ { \mathrm { min } } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 } \left( \frac { x } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 81 } x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 2 \pi } { 27 } + 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 44 } { 189 } + 1 \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 233 } { 189 } \right) = \frac { 466 } { 567 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}

question_html: The sides of an equilateral triangle are increasing at the rate of 2 cm/sec. The rate at which the area increases, when side is 10 cm is:

options.explanation: Let the side of an equilateral triangle be x cm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 2 cm/s (given) Differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. 't', we get {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 2 x \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3} x d x}{d t}{/tex} When x = 10 {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{10 \sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 2=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

If the top of the ladder slides downwards at the rate of 10 cm/sec, then the rate at which the angle between the floor and the ladder is decreasing when lower end of ladder is 2 metres from the wall is:
question_html: A ladder, 5 meter long, standing on a horizontal floor, leans against a vertical wall. If the top of the ladder slides downwards at the rate of 10 cm/sec, then the rate at which the angle between the floor and the ladder is decreasing when lower end of ladder is 2 metres from the wall is:

options.explanation: Let the angle between floor and the ladder be {tex}\theta{/tex} Let at any time 't' AB = x cm and BC = y cm {tex}\therefore\sin \theta=\frac{x}{500}{/tex} and {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{y}{500}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 500 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 500 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Also it is given that {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 10 cm/s {tex}\Rightarrow 500 \cdot \cos \theta \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d t}{/tex} = 10 cm {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{1}{50 \cos \theta}{/tex} For y = 2 m = 200 cm, {tex}\frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{1}{50 \cdot \frac{y}{500}}=\frac{10}{y}=\frac{10}{200}=\frac{1}{20} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

question_text: The equation of normal to the curve 3x 2 - y 2 = 8 which is parallel to the line x + 3y = 8 is
question_html: The equation of normal to the curve 3x2 - y2 = 8 which is parallel to the line x + 3y = 8 is

options.explanation: Given equation of the curve is 3x 2 - y 2 = 5 .... (i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t, we get {tex}6 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x}{y}{/tex} , which is slope of tangent at any point on the curve {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of normal at any point on the curve is {tex}-\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{-y}{3 x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad-\frac{y}{3 x}=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x .... (ii) From (i) and (ii), we get 3x 2 - x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\pm 2{/tex} For x = 2, y = 2 [using (iii)] and for x = -2, y = -2 [using (iii)] Thus, the points on the curve at which normal to the curve are parallel to the line x + 3y are (2, 2) and (-2, -2). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equations of normal are {tex}y-2=-\frac{1}{3}(x-2){/tex} and y + 2 {tex}=-\frac{1}{3}(x+2){/tex} or 3y + x = 8 and 3y + x = -8

question_text: If y = x 4 - 10 and if x changes from 2 to 1.99, what is the change in y
question_html: If y = x4 - 10 and if x changes from 2 to 1.99, what is the change in y

options.explanation: We have, y = x 4 - 10 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 4x 3 and {tex}\Delta x{/tex} = 2.00 - 1.99 = 0.01 {tex}\therefore \Delta y=\frac{d y}{d x} \times \Delta x=4 x^{3} \times \Delta x{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 = 0.32 So, the approximate change in y is 0.32

question_html: The equation of tangent to the curve y (1 + x2) = 2 - x, where it crosses x-axis is:

options.explanation: We have, equation of the curve y(1 + x 2 ) = 2 - x ...(i) It is given that the curve crosses x-axis Putting y = 0 in equation (i), we get {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0(1 + x 2 ) = 2 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 So the curves passes through the point (2, 0) Now differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\therefore y \times(0+2 x)+\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1-2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(2,0)}=\frac{-1-2 \times 0}{1+2^{2}}=-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} = slope of tangent to the curve {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent of the curve passing through (2, 0) is y - 0 = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} (x - 2) or x + 5y = 2

question_text: The points at which the tangents to the curve y = x 3 - 12x + 18 are parallel to x-axis are:
question_html: The points at which the tangents to the curve y = x3 - 12x + 18 are parallel to x-axis are:

options.explanation: The given equation of curve is y = x 3 - 12x + 18 {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 3x 2 - 12 If tangent to the curve are parallel to the x-axis {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 - 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{12}{3}=4{/tex} {tex}\therefore x=\pm 2{/tex} For x = 2, y = 2 3 - 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 18 = 2 and for x = -2, y = (-2) 3 - 12(-2) + 18 = 34 So, the points are (2, 2) and (-2, 34)

question_html: The slope of the tangent to the curve x = t2 + 3t - 8, y = 2t2 - 2t - 5 at the point (2,-1) is

options.explanation: It is given that curve x = t 2 + 3t - 8, y = 2t 2 - 2t - 5 Then, {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=2 t+3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=4 t-2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{4 t-2}{2 t+3}{/tex} The given point is (2, -1) So, At x = 2, we get t 2 + 3t - 8 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 + 3t - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (t - 2)(t + 5) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 2 and -5 At y = -1, we get 2t 2 - 2t - 5 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t 2 - 2t - 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(t 2 - t - 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (t - 2)(t + 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 2 and -1 Therefore, the common value of t is 2. Hence, the slope of the tangent to the given curve at point (2, - 1) is {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{t=2}=\frac{4(2)-2}{2(2)+3}=\frac{8-2}{4+3}=\frac{6}{7}{/tex}

question_html: The two curves x3 - 3xy2 + 2 = 0 and 3x2y - y3 - 2 = 0 intersect at an angle of

options.explanation: Given equation of curves are x 3 - 3xy 2 + 2 = 0 ...(i) and 3x 2 y - y 3 - 2 = 0 ...(ii) Differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}3 x^{2}-3 x \cdot 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-3 y^{2} \cdot 1+0=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Differentiating equation (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}3 x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+6 x y-3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+0=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} Now {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-1{/tex} Hence, the curves intersect at right angle.

question_html: The function f(x) = 4 sin3x - 6 sin2x + 12 sinx + 100 is strictly

options.explanation: We have, f(x) = 4 sin 3 x - 6 sin 2 x + 12 sin x + 100 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 12 sin 2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} cos x - 12 sin x {tex}\cdot{/tex} cos x + 12 cos x = 12 cos x [sin 2 x - sin x + 1] = 12 cos x [sin 2 x + (1 - sinx)] Now 1 - sin x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 and sin 2 x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} sin 2 x + 1 - sin x > 0 Hence f'(x) > 0, when cos x > 0 i.e., {tex}x \in\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} So, f(x) is increasing when {tex}x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} and f'(x) < 0, when cos x < 0 i.e., {tex}x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing when {tex}x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}

question_html: The smallest value of the polynomial x3 - 18x2 + 96x in [0, 9] is

question_text: For the curve y = 5x - 2x 3 , if x increases at the rate of 2 units/sec, then how fast is the slope of curve changing when x = 3?
question_html: For the curve y = 5x - 2x3, if x increases at the rate of 2 units/sec, then how fast is the slope of curve changing when x = 3?
solution_text: Slope of curve = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 5 - 6x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=-12 x \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -12 (3) (2) = -72 units/sec. Thus, slope of curve is decreasing at the rate of 72 units/sec when x is increasing at the rate of 2 units/sec.

Slope of curve = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 5 - 6x2
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=-12 x \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
= -12 (3) (2)
= -72 units/sec. 
Thus, slope of curve is decreasing at the rate of 72 units/sec when x is increasing at the rate of 2 units/sec.

question_text: Water is dripping out from a conical funnel of semi-vertical angle {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} at the uniform rate of 2 cm 2 /sec in the surface area, through a tiny hole at the vertex of the bottom. When the slant height of cone is 4 cm, find the rate of decrease of the slant height of water.
question_html: Water is dripping out from a conical funnel of semi-vertical angle {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} at the uniform rate of 2 cm2/sec in the surface area, through a tiny hole at the vertex of the bottom. When the slant height of cone is 4 cm, find the rate of decrease of the slant height of water.
solution_text: If s represents the surface area, then {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 2c{m^2}/\sec {/tex} Also, on using trigonometric ratios, radius of cone can be taken as {tex}r = l\sin \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi rl = \pi l.\sin \frac{\pi }{4}l = \frac{\pi }{{\sqrt 2 }}{l^2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{\sqrt 2 }}l.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \sqrt 2 \pi l.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \pi .l}}.\frac{ds}{dt} {/tex} when {tex}l = 4cm,\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \pi .4}}.2 = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 2 \pi }} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{4\pi }}cm/s{/tex} Thus, rate of decrease of slant height of water is {tex}\frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{4\pi }}cm/\sec {/tex} .
solution_html:
If s represents the surface area, then
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 2c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
Also, on using trigonometric ratios, radius of cone can be taken as
{tex}r = l\sin \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
{tex}s = \pi rl = \pi l.\sin \frac{\pi }{4}l = \frac{\pi }{{\sqrt 2 }}{l^2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{\sqrt 2 }}l.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \sqrt 2 \pi l.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \pi .l}}.\frac{ds}{dt} {/tex}
when {tex}l = 4cm,\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \pi .4}}.2 = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 2 \pi }} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{4\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
Thus, rate of decrease of slant height of water is {tex}\frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{4\pi }}cm/\sec {/tex}.

question_text: Find the angle of intersection of the curves {tex}{y^2} = x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} = y{/tex} .
question_html: Find the angle of intersection of the curves {tex}{y^2} = x{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} = y{/tex}.
solution_text: Solving the given equations, we have y 2 = x and x 2 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 = x or x 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x (x 3 - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0,x = 1 Therefore, y = 0, y = 1 i.e. points of intersection are (0, 0) and (1, 1) Further {tex}{y^2} = x \Rightarrow 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2y}}{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} = y \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2x{/tex} At (0, 0), the slope of the tangent to the curve y 2 = x is parallel to y-axis and the tangent to the curve x 2 = y is parallel to x-axis. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} angle of intersection {tex} = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} At (1, 1) slope of the tangent to the curve y 2 = x is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and that of x 2 = y is 2. {tex}\tan \theta = \left| {\frac{{2 - \frac{1}{2}}}{{1 + 1}}} \right| = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{3}{4}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Solving the given equations, we have y2 = x and x2 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4 = x or x4 - x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x (x3 - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0,x = 1
Therefore, y = 0, y = 1
i.e. points of intersection are (0, 0) and (1, 1)
Further {tex}{y^2} = x \Rightarrow 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2y}}{/tex}
and {tex}{x^2} = y \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2x{/tex}
At (0, 0), the slope of the tangent to the curve y2 = x is parallel to y-axis and the tangent to the curve x2 = y is parallel to x-axis.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} angle of intersection {tex} = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
At (1, 1) slope of the tangent to the curve y2 = x is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and that of x2 = y is 2.
{tex}\tan \theta = \left| {\frac{{2 - \frac{1}{2}}}{{1 + 1}}} \right| = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{3}{4}} \right){/tex}

question_text: Prove that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = \tan x - 4x{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{3},\frac{\pi }{3}} \right){/tex} .
question_html: Prove that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = \tan x - 4x{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{3},\frac{\pi }{3}} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Given function, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \tan x - 4x \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = {\sec ^2}x - 4{/tex} When {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3} < x < \frac{\pi }{3},1 < \sec x < 2{/tex} Therefore, {tex}1 < {\sec ^2}x < 4 \Rightarrow - 3 < \left( {{{\sec }^2}x - 4} \right) < 0{/tex} Thus for {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3} < x < \frac{\pi }{3},f'\left( x \right) < 0{/tex} Hence, f is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{3},\frac{\pi }{3}} \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Given function, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \tan x - 4x \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = {\sec ^2}x - 4{/tex}
When {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3} < x < \frac{\pi }{3},1 < \sec x < 2{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}1 < {\sec ^2}x < 4 \Rightarrow - 3 < \left( {{{\sec }^2}x - 4} \right) < 0{/tex}
Thus for {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3} < x < \frac{\pi }{3},f'\left( x \right) < 0{/tex}
Hence, f is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{3},\frac{\pi }{3}} \right){/tex}.

question_html: Determine for which values of x, the function {tex}y = {x^4} - \frac{{4{x^3}}}{3}{/tex} is increasing and for which values, it is decreasing.

question_text: Show that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} has neither maxima nor minima.
question_html: Show that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} has neither maxima nor minima.
solution_text: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 36x + 27 = 3\left( {4{x^2} - 12x + 9} \right) = 3{\left( {2x - 3} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} (Critical point) Since {tex}f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x < \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and for all {tex}x > \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a point of inflexion i.e., neither a point of maxima nor a point of minima. {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the only critical point, and f has neither maxima nor minima.
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex}

{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 36x + 27 = 3\left( {4{x^2} - 12x + 9} \right) = 3{\left( {2x - 3} \right)^2}{/tex}

{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}(Critical point)

Since {tex}f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x < \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and for all {tex}x > \frac{3}{2}{/tex}

Hence {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a point of inflexion i.e., neither a point of maxima nor a point of minima.

{tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the only critical point, and f has neither maxima nor minima.

solution_text: Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sqrt x {/tex} Using {tex}f\left( {x + \Delta x} \right) = f\left( x \right) + \Delta x.f'\left( x \right),{/tex} taking, x = 0.09, and {tex}\Delta x = - 0.008{/tex} we get {tex}f\left( {0.09 - 0.008} \right) = f\left( {0.09} \right) + \left( { - 0.008} \right).f'\left( {0.09} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {0.082} = \sqrt {0.09} - 0.008.\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {0.09} }}} \right) = 0.3 - \frac{{0.008}}{{0.6}}{/tex} = 0.3 – 0.0133 = 0.2867.

question_text: Find the condition for the curves {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1;xy = {c^2}{/tex} to intersect orthogonally.
question_html: Find the condition for the curves {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1;xy = {c^2}{/tex} to intersect orthogonally.
solution_text: Let the curves intersect at {tex}\left( {{x_1},{y_1}} \right){/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{2x}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{2y}}{{{b^2}}}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{b^2}x}}{{{a^2}y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Slope of tangent at the point of intersection is {tex}{{m_1}} = \frac{{{b^2}{x_1}}}{{{a^2}{y_1}}}{/tex} Again, {tex}xy = {c^2} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x} \Rightarrow {m_2} = \frac{{ - {y_1}}}{{{x_1}}}{/tex} . For orthoganality, {tex}{m_1} \times {m_2} = - 1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}{x_1}}}{{{a^2}{y_1}}}×\frac{-y_1}{x_1}=-1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = 1\,\,or\,\,{a^2} - {b^2} = 0{/tex} . Which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Let the curves intersect at {tex}\left( {{x_1},{y_1}} \right){/tex}.
Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{2x}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{2y}}{{{b^2}}}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{b^2}x}}{{{a^2}y}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Slope of tangent at the point of intersection is {tex}{{m_1}} = \frac{{{b^2}{x_1}}}{{{a^2}{y_1}}}{/tex}
Again, {tex}xy = {c^2} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y = 0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x} \Rightarrow {m_2} = \frac{{ - {y_1}}}{{{x_1}}}{/tex}.
For orthoganality, {tex}{m_1} \times {m_2} = - 1 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}{x_1}}}{{{a^2}{y_1}}}×\frac{-y_1}{x_1}=-1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = 1\,\,or\,\,{a^2} - {b^2} = 0{/tex}.

Which is the required solution.

question_text: Find all the point of local maxima and local minima of the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = - \frac{3}{4}{x^4} - 8{x^3} - \frac{{45}}{2}{x^2} + 105{/tex} .
question_html: Find all the point of local maxima and local minima of the function{tex}f\left( x \right) = - \frac{3}{4}{x^4} - 8{x^3} - \frac{{45}}{2}{x^2} + 105{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}f'\left( x \right) = - 3{x^3} - 24{x^2} - 45x{/tex} {tex} = - 3x\left( {{x^2} + 8x + 15} \right) = - 3x\left( {x + 5} \right)\left( {x + 3} \right){/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 5,x = - 3,x = 0{/tex} {tex}f''\left( x \right) = - 9{x^2} - 48x - 45{/tex} {tex}= - 3\left( {3{x^2} + 16x + 15} \right){/tex} {tex}f''\left( 0 \right) = - 45 < 0{/tex} . Therefore, x = 0 is point of local maxima {tex}f''\left( { - 3} \right) = 18 > 0{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}x = - 3{/tex} is point of local minima {tex}f''\left( { - 5} \right) = - 30 < 0{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}x = - 5{/tex} is point of local maxima.
solution_html:
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = - 3{x^3} - 24{x^2} - 45x{/tex}

{tex} = - 3x\left( {{x^2} + 8x + 15} \right) = - 3x\left( {x + 5} \right)\left( {x + 3} \right){/tex}

{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 5,x = - 3,x = 0{/tex}

{tex}f''\left( x \right) = - 9{x^2} - 48x - 45{/tex}

{tex}= - 3\left( {3{x^2} + 16x + 15} \right){/tex}

{tex}f''\left( 0 \right) = - 45 < 0{/tex}. Therefore, x = 0 is point of local maxima

{tex}f''\left( { - 3} \right) = 18 > 0{/tex}. Therefore, {tex}x = - 3{/tex} is point of local minima

{tex}f''\left( { - 5} \right) = - 30 < 0{/tex}. Therefore, {tex}x = - 5{/tex} is point of local maxima.

question_text: Show that the local maximum value of {tex}x + \frac{1}{x}{/tex} is less than local minimum value.
question_html: Show that the local maximum value of {tex}x + \frac{1}{x}{/tex} is less than local minimum value.
solution_text: Let {tex}y = x + \frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 - \frac{1}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow {x^2} = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{2}{x^3}{/tex} ,therefore, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}\left( {at\,x = 1} \right)=2 > 0{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}\left( {at\,x = - 1} \right)=-2 < 0{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Hence local maximum value of y is at x = - 1 and the local maximum value = - 2. Local minimum value of y is at x = 1 and local minimum value = 2. Therefore, local maximum value (- 2) is less than local minimum value 2.
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = x + \frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 - \frac{1}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow {x^2} = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{2}{x^3}{/tex} ,therefore, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}\left( {at\,x = 1} \right)=2 > 0{/tex} and  

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}\left( {at\,x = - 1} \right)=-2 < 0{/tex}{tex}{/tex}

Hence local maximum value of y is at x = - 1 and the local maximum value = - 2.

Local minimum value of y is at x = 1 and local minimum value  = 2.

Therefore, local maximum value (- 2) is less than local minimum value 2.

question_text: Water is dripping out at a steady rate of {tex}1cu\,cm/\sec {/tex} through a tiny hole at the vertex of the conical vessel, whose axis is vertical. When the slant height of water in the vessel is 4 cm, find the rate of decrease of slant height, where the vertical angle of the conical vessel is {tex}\frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} .
question_html: Water is dripping out at a steady rate of {tex}1cu\,cm/\sec {/tex} through a tiny hole at the vertex of the conical vessel, whose axis is vertical. When the slant height of water in the vessel is 4 cm, find the rate of decrease of slant height, where the vertical angle of the conical vessel is {tex}\frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}.
solution_text: Given that {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 1c{m^3}/s{/tex} where v is the volume of water in the conical vessel. From the Fig, {tex}l = 4cm,h = l\cos \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}l{/tex} and {tex}r = l\sin \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{l}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h = \frac{\pi }{3}\frac{{{l^2}}}{4}\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}l = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{24}}{l^3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 \pi }}{8}{l^2}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex} Therefore, Rate of decrease of slant height, when slant height is 4 units. {tex} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 \pi }}{8}16.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 \pi }}cm/s{/tex} Therefore, the rate of decrease of slant height {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 \pi }}cm/s{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 1c{m^3}/s{/tex} where v is the volume of water in the conical vessel.
From the Fig, {tex}l = 4cm,h = l\cos \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}l{/tex} and {tex}r = l\sin \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{l}{2}{/tex}.
Therefore, {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h = \frac{\pi }{3}\frac{{{l^2}}}{4}\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}l = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{24}}{l^3}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 \pi }}{8}{l^2}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex} 

Therefore, Rate of decrease of slant height, when slant height is 4 units. {tex} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 \pi }}{8}16.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 \pi }}cm/s{/tex} 
Therefore, the rate of decrease of slant height {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 \pi }}cm/s{/tex}.

question_html: Find the equation of tangents to the curve y = cos(x + y), – 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex} that are parallel to the line x + 2y = 0.
solution_text: Given that {tex}y = \cos \left( {x + y} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {x + y} \right)\,\left[ {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{/tex} Since tangent is parallel to x + 2y = 0, therefore slope of tangent {tex}= - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}- \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1{/tex} ...(ii) Since {tex}\cos \left( {x + y} \right) = y{/tex} and {tex}\sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) + {\sin ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) = {y^2} + 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {y^2} + 1{/tex} or y = 0. Therefore, cos x = 0 Therefore, {tex}x = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\pi }{2},n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2..{/tex} Thus, {tex}x = \pm \frac{\pi }{2}, \pm \frac{{3\pi }}{2},{/tex} but {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2},x = \frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2}{/tex} satisfy equation (ii) Hence, the points are {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} . Therefore, equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = 1\left( {x - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y - \pi = 0,{/tex} and equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {x + \frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y + 3\pi = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}y = \cos \left( {x + y} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {x + y} \right)\,\left[ {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{/tex}
Since tangent is parallel to x + 2y = 0, therefore slope of tangent {tex}= - \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}- \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1{/tex} ...(ii)
Since {tex}\cos \left( {x + y} \right) = y{/tex} and {tex}\sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) + {\sin ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) = {y^2} + 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {y^2} + 1{/tex} or y = 0.
Therefore, cos x = 0
Therefore, {tex}x = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\pi }{2},n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2..{/tex}
Thus, {tex}x = \pm \frac{\pi }{2}, \pm \frac{{3\pi }}{2},{/tex} but {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2},x = \frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2}{/tex} satisfy equation (ii)
Hence, the points are {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex}.
Therefore, equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = 1\left( {x - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y - \pi = 0,{/tex} and equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {x + \frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y + 3\pi = 0{/tex}

question_text: Find the angle of intersection of the curves y 2 = 4ax and x 2 = 4by.
question_html: Find the angle of intersection of the curves y2 = 4ax and x2 = 4by.
solution_text: Given that y 2 = 4ax...(i) and x 2 = 4by...(ii). Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4 b}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 4ax {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 = 64 ab 2 x or x(x 3 - 64 ab 2 ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, x = {tex}4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} Therefore, the points of intersection are (0, 0) and {tex}\left(4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}, 4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}\right){/tex} Again, {tex}y^{2}=4 a x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 a}{2 y}=\frac{2 a}{y}{/tex} and {tex}x^{2}=4 b y \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{4 b}=\frac{x}{2 b}{/tex} Therefore, at (0, 0) the tangent to the curve y 2 = 4ax is parallel to y-axis and tangent to the curve x 2 = 4by is parallel to x-axis. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Angle between curves = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} At {tex}\left(4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}, 4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}\right),{/tex} m 1 (slope of the tangent to the curve (i)) = {tex}2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 a}{4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}},{/tex} m 2 (slope of the tangent to the curve (ii)) = {tex}\frac{4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}}{2 b}=2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} }{| 1+2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}\right|=\frac{3 a^{\frac{1}{3}} \cdot b^{\frac{1}{3}}}{2\left(a^{\frac{2}{3}}+b^{\frac{2}{3}}\right)}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 a^{\frac{1}{3}} \cdot b^{\frac{1}{3}}}{2\left(a^{\frac{2}{3}}+b^{\frac{2}{3}}\right)}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given that y2 = 4ax...(i) and x2 = 4by...(ii).
Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4 b}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 4ax {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4 = 64 ab2x
or x(x3 - 64 ab2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, x = {tex}4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
Therefore, the points of intersection are (0, 0) and {tex}\left(4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}, 4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}\right){/tex}
Again, {tex}y^{2}=4 a x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 a}{2 y}=\frac{2 a}{y}{/tex} and {tex}x^{2}=4 b y \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{4 b}=\frac{x}{2 b}{/tex}
Therefore, at (0, 0) the tangent to the curve y2 = 4ax is parallel to y-axis and tangent to the curve x2 = 4by is parallel to x-axis.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Angle between curves = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
At {tex}\left(4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}, 4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}\right),{/tex} m1 (slope of the tangent to the curve (i)) = {tex}2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 a}{4 a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}},{/tex} m2 (slope of the tangent to the curve (ii)) = {tex}\frac{4 a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}}{2 b}=2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} }{| 1+2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}\right|=\frac{3 a^{\frac{1}{3}} \cdot b^{\frac{1}{3}}}{2\left(a^{\frac{2}{3}}+b^{\frac{2}{3}}\right)}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 a^{\frac{1}{3}} \cdot b^{\frac{1}{3}}}{2\left(a^{\frac{2}{3}}+b^{\frac{2}{3}}\right)}\right){/tex}

question_text: Find the maximum and minimum values of f(x) = secx + log cos 2 x, 0 < x < 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum values of f(x) = secx + log cos2x, 0 < x < 2{tex}\pi{/tex}
solution_text: f(x) = sec x + 2 log ((cos x)) Therefore, f'(x) = sec x tan x - 2 tan x = tan x (sec x - 2) f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 0 or sec x = 2 or cos x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, possible values of x are, x = 0, or x = {tex}\pi{/tex} and x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} Again, f'(x) = sec 2 x (sec x - 2) + tan x (sec x tan x) = sec 3 x + sec x tan 2 x - 2sec 2 x = sec x (sec 2 x + tan 2 x - 2secx) We note that f'(0) = 1 (1 + 0 - 2) = -1 < 0. Therefore, x = 0 is a point of maxima f'( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) = -1 (1 + 0 + 2) = -3 < 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\pi{/tex} is a point of maxima {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} = 2 (4 + 3 - 4) = 6 > 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} is a point of minima {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = 2 (4 + 3 - 4) = 6 > 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} is a point of minima Maximum Value of y at x = 0 is 1 + 0 = 1 Maximum Value of y at x = {tex}\pi{/tex} is -1 + 0 = -1 Minimum Value of y at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} is 2 + 2 log {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 (1 - log2) Minimum Value of y at x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} is 2 + 2 log {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 (1 - log2)
solution_html:
f(x) = sec x + 2 log ((cos x))
Therefore, f'(x) = sec x tan x - 2 tan x = tan x (sec x - 2)
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 0 or sec x = 2 or cos x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, possible values of x are, x = 0, or x = {tex}\pi{/tex} and x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex}
Again, f'(x) = sec2x (sec x - 2) + tan x (sec x tan x)
= sec3x + sec x tan2x - 2sec2x
= sec x (sec2x + tan2x - 2secx)
We note that
f'(0) = 1 (1 + 0 - 2) = -1 < 0. Therefore, x = 0 is a point of maxima
f'({tex}\pi{/tex}) = -1 (1 + 0 + 2) = -3 < 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\pi{/tex} is a point of maxima
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} = 2 (4 + 3 - 4) = 6 > 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} is a point of minima
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = 2 (4 + 3 - 4) = 6 > 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} is a point of minima
 Maximum Value of y at x = 0 is 1 + 0 = 1
Maximum Value of y at x = {tex}\pi{/tex} is -1 + 0 = -1
Minimum Value of y at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} is 2 + 2 log{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 (1 - log2)
Minimum Value of y at x = {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} is 2 + 2 log{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 (1 - log2)

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex} .
question_html: Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex}.
solution_text: Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig. The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say) Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum. Maximum area {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig.

The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say)
Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} 
Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} 
Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum.
Maximum area  {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.

question_text: Find the difference between the greatest and least values of the function f(x) = sin2x - x, on {tex}\left[ { - \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the difference between the greatest and least values of the function f(x) = sin2x - x, on {tex}\left[ { - \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin 2x - x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 2\cos 2x - 1{/tex} Therefore, {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2x = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 2x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi }{3} \Rightarrow x = - \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}f\left( { - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \sin \left( { - \pi } \right) + \frac{\pi }{2} = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}f\left( { - \frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( { - \frac{{2\pi }}{6}} \right) + \frac{\pi }{6} = - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{6}} \right) - \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( \pi \right) - \frac{\pi }{6} = - \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Clearly, {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} is the greatest value and {tex} - \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} is the least. Therefore, difference {tex} = \frac{\pi }{2} + \frac{\pi }{2} = \pi {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin 2x - x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 2\cos 2x - 1{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2x = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 2x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{3}{/tex}or {tex}\frac{\pi }{3} \Rightarrow x = - \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

{tex}f\left( { - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \sin \left( { - \pi } \right) + \frac{\pi }{2} = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex}f\left( { - \frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( { - \frac{{2\pi }}{6}} \right) + \frac{\pi }{6} = - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

{tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{6}} \right) - \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

{tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \sin \left( \pi \right) - \frac{\pi }{6} = - \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Clearly, {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} is the greatest value and {tex} - \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} is the least.
 Therefore, difference {tex} = \frac{\pi }{2} + \frac{\pi }{2} = \pi {/tex}

question_text: An isosceles triangle of vertical angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} is inscribed in a circle of radius a. Show that the area of the triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} .
question_html: An isosceles triangle of vertical angle 2{tex}\theta{/tex} is inscribed in a circle of radius a. Show that the area of the triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let ABC be an isosceles triangle inscribed in the circle with radius a such that AB = AC. {tex}AD = AO + OD = a + a\cos 2\theta{/tex} and {tex}BC = 2BD = 2a\sin 2\theta{/tex} (see fig.) Therefore, area of the triangle ABC i.e. {tex}\triangle = \frac{1}{2}BC.AD{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}2a\sin 2\theta .\left( {a + a\cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= {a^2}\sin 2\theta \left( {1 + \cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \triangle = {a^2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{1}{2}{a^2}\sin 4\theta {/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 2{a^2}\cos 2\theta + 2{a^2}\cos 4\theta {/tex} {tex} = 2{a^2}\left( {\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = - \cos 4\theta = \cos \left( {\pi - 4\theta } \right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}2\theta = \pi - 4\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}\Delta }}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 2{a^2}\left( { - 2\sin 2\theta - 4\sin 4\theta } \right) < 0\left( {at\ \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex} Therefore, Area of triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta = \frac{\pi }{6}.{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABC be an isosceles triangle inscribed in the circle with radius a such that AB = AC.
{tex}AD = AO + OD = a + a\cos 2\theta{/tex}and {tex}BC = 2BD = 2a\sin 2\theta{/tex} (see fig.)

Therefore, area of the triangle ABC i.e. {tex}\triangle = \frac{1}{2}BC.AD{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}2a\sin 2\theta .\left( {a + a\cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex}= {a^2}\sin 2\theta \left( {1 + \cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \triangle = {a^2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{1}{2}{a^2}\sin 4\theta {/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 2{a^2}\cos 2\theta + 2{a^2}\cos 4\theta {/tex} 
{tex} = 2{a^2}\left( {\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = - \cos 4\theta = \cos \left( {\pi - 4\theta } \right){/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}2\theta = \pi - 4\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}\Delta }}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 2{a^2}\left( { - 2\sin 2\theta - 4\sin 4\theta } \right) < 0\left( {at\ \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex}
Therefore, Area of triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta = \frac{\pi }{6}.{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{4}+a\left(-\frac{a}{2}\right)+25=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a=\pm 10{/tex}
Hence, the values of a are {tex}\pm 10{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x^{2}+2 x+1},{/tex} then its maximum value is ________.
question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x^{2}+2 x+1},{/tex} then its maximum value is ________.
solution_text: For f to be maximum, 4x 2 + 2x + 1 should be minimum i.e. 4x 2 + 2x + 1 = 4 {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}+\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} giving the minimum value of 4x 2 + 2x + 1 = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} Hence maximum value of {tex}f=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
For f to be maximum, 4x2 + 2x + 1 should be minimum i.e.
4x2 + 2x + 1 = 4 {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}+\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} giving the minimum value of 4x2 + 2x + 1 = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Hence maximum value of {tex}f=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
